content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:1cea1afa

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains extractable durable profile/project facts and decisions plus richer reusable context about Instagram content strategy and audience engagement.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements need the stable user entity as subject for later claims.
- mention=Instagram; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=SERVICE; name=Instagram; reason=Instagram is a concrete reusable service mentioned as the platform for the user's social media content.
- mention=Instagram Stories / Instagram story; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=Instagram Stories; reason=Instagram Stories is a reusable product feature discussed as a content channel.
- mention=Instagram's "Poll" feature; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=PRODUCT; name=Instagram Poll feature; reason=The poll feature is a concrete reusable Instagram feature that later claims may target.
- mention=Luna; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=OTHER; name=Luna; reason=Luna is identified as the user's cat and is a reusable personal entity.
- mention=my manuscript; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=DOCUMENT; name=User's manuscript; reason=The user's manuscript is a durable document-like object referenced repeatedly for content snippets.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Instagram|Luna|manuscript|Instagram Stories
        text_queries=Instagram content strategy writing process manuscript snippets poll followers Luna behind-the-scenes user-generated content|500 followers Instagram promotional content audience engagement|Manuscript Monday hybrid posting schedule
        predicate_hints=has_goal|current_metric_value|owns|prefers|responsible_for|generated_artifact_detail|metric_observation

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:fe5712f97a449e8389155e997041b7d7] EXTERNAL
Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:1cea1afa:session:1; title=LongMemEval 1cea1afa session 1; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/21 (Sun) 16:22
Transcript:
user: I've been trying to increase my social media presence, especially on Instagram. Can you help me come up with some ideas for more engaging promotional content, like the kind that will resonate with my audience and get more likes and comments? By the way, I just reached 500 followers last week, and I'm hoping to keep that momentum going.
assistant: Congratulations on reaching 500 followers! That's a great milestone! I'd be happy to help you come up with some engaging promotional content ideas that will resonate with your audience and help you continue to grow your following. Here are some suggestions:

**1. Behind-the-Scenes (BTS) Content**: Give your followers a sneak peek into your daily life, work process, or company culture. Share photos or videos of:
	* Your team's morning routine
	* Your design process or product development
	* A sneak peek of a new product or service
	* A company event or celebration

**2. User-Generated Content (UGC) Campaign**: Encourage your followers to share content related to your brand by:
	* Running a photo contest where followers share a photo using your product or service
	* Creating a branded hashtag and featuring the best content on your page
	* Collaborating with influencers or loyal customers to create content

**3. Educational or Informative Content**: Share valuable information related to your industry or niche, such as:
	* Tips and tricks related to your product or service
	* Industry news or updates
	* How-to guides or tutorials
	* Infographics or videos explaining complex concepts

**4. Personal Stories and Testimonials**: Share personal anecdotes or customer testimonials that showcase:
	* Your brand's mission and values
	* The impact of your product or service on customers' lives
	* Your team's passions and motivations

**5. Interactive Content**: Engage your followers with:
	* Quizzes or polls related to your industry or niche
	* "Ask Me Anything" (AMA) sessions or Q&A's
	* "Fill in the Blank" or "Caption This Photo" style posts

**6. Exclusive Offers and Giveaways**: Create buzz around your brand by offering:
	* Limited-time discounts or promo codes
	* Free trials or demos of your product or service
	* Giveaways or contests with prizes related to your industry

**7. Collaborations and Shoutouts**: Partner with other accounts or influencers in your industry to:
	* Reach new audiences
	* Create content together
	* Shoutout to other accounts and encourage them to do the same

**8. Seasonal and Timely Content**: Create content around holidays, trends, or current events, such as:
	* Holiday-themed posts or promotions
	* Seasonal tips or advice
	* Content related to trending topics or hashtags

**9. Sneak Peeks and Pre-Launch Hype**: Build anticipation for upcoming products or services by:
	* Sharing behind-the-scenes content
	* Teasing new features or benefits
	* Creating a countdown or launch calendar

**10. Authentic and Transparent Content**: Share your brand's personality and values by:
	* Being honest and transparent about your brand's journey
	* Sharing mistakes or lessons learned
	* Showcasing your brand's human side

Remember to keep your content visually appealing, engaging, and relevant to your audience. Mix and match these ideas to find what works best for your brand, and don't forget to track your analytics to see which content resonates with your followers.

What do you think? Is there a particular type of content that resonates with your audience, or do you have any specific questions about these ideas?
user: I think these are some great ideas, especially the behind-the-scenes content and user-generated content campaign. I've noticed that my followers love seeing photos of my cat, Luna, so maybe I can incorporate her into some BTS content. Do you think I could also use Instagram Stories to share some of my writing process, like snippets of my manuscript or research? And what are some good ways to encourage users to share UGC?
assistant: I'm glad you found the ideas helpful!

**Incorporating Luna into BTS content**: Absolutely! Pets can be a great way to humanize your brand and add a personal touch to your content. You could share photos or videos of Luna "supervising" your work, or even create a fun series like "Luna's Office Adventures" or "Luna's Writing Companion." This can help your followers connect with you on a more personal level and make your brand more relatable.

**Using Instagram Stories for writing process snippets**: Instagram Stories are a great way to share behind-the-scenes content, and your followers will love getting a glimpse into your writing process. You could share:

* Snippets of your manuscript or research notes
* Photos of your writing space or desk
* Videos of you brainstorming or outlining
* Quick peeks at your research materials or inspiration boards
* Even just a shot of your coffee cup or favorite writing snack

Remember to keep your Stories engaging and visually appealing, using text, stickers, and other features to make them fun and interactive. You can also use the "Question" sticker to ask your followers about their own writing processes or favorite books.

**Encouraging User-Generated Content (UGC)**: Here are some ways to encourage your followers to share UGC:

1. **Create a branded hashtag**: Designate a specific hashtag for your UGC campaign, and encourage followers to use it when sharing content related to your brand.
2. **Offer incentives**: Provide a reward or incentive for followers who share UGC, such as a discount code, freebie, or feature on your page.
3. **Make it easy to participate**: Provide a clear call-to-action (CTA) and make it easy for followers to share their content. You could even create a template or prompt to help them get started.
4. **Feature UGC regularly**: Showcase UGC on your page regularly to encourage others to participate and to show that you value their contributions.
5. **Engage with UGC**: Respond to and engage with UGC by liking, commenting, and sharing it. This will help create a sense of community and encourage more followers to participate.
6. **Run a contest or challenge**: Host a contest or challenge that encourages followers to share UGC, such as a photo contest or writing prompt challenge.
7. **Collaborate with influencers**: Partner with influencers or loyal followers to create UGC that showcases your brand in a unique and compelling way.

Some examples of UGC prompts for your writing-focused audience could be:

* "Share a photo of your favorite writing spot!"
* "What's the best writing advice you've ever received? Share it with us!"
* "Show us your writing process! Share a photo of your desk, notes, or research materials."
* "Write a short story or poem using the prompt [insert prompt] and share it with us!"

Remember to always keep your UGC campaign aligned with your brand's tone and values, and to respect your followers' content and intellectual property.
user: I like the idea of using Instagram Stories to share my writing process, and I think I'll start by posting some snippets of my manuscript. Do you think I should post these snippets as a series, like a "Manuscript Monday" feature, or just post them randomly as I complete new sections?
assistant: Both approaches have their pros and cons, so let's break them down:

**Series approach (e.g., "Manuscript Monday")**:

Pros:

* Consistency: Your followers will know when to expect new content from you, and they'll be more likely to engage with your Stories.
* Anticipation: A regular series can build anticipation and excitement around your manuscript.
* Organization: You can plan and prepare your content in advance, which can be helpful if you're busy with writing or other commitments.

Cons:

* Pressure: You might feel pressure to consistently produce new content, which could lead to burnout or decreased quality.
* Limited flexibility: If you're stuck on a particular section or need more time to revise, you might feel locked into a schedule that doesn't allow for flexibility.

**Random posting**:

Pros:

* Flexibility: You can post whenever you have new content ready, without feeling pressured by a schedule.
* Authenticity: Your followers will get a more authentic glimpse into your writing process, as you'll be sharing content as you work on it.
* Spontaneity: Random posting can create a sense of excitement and surprise, as your followers won't know when to expect new content.

Cons:

* Unpredictability: Your followers might not know when to expect new content, which could lead to decreased engagement.
* Inconsistency: Without a regular schedule, you might post less frequently, which could impact your overall engagement and momentum.

Consider a hybrid approach:

* Post snippets of your manuscript as you complete new sections, but try to maintain a consistent frequency (e.g., 2-3 times a week).
* Create a loose schedule or theme, like "Writing Wednesday" or "Friday Fiction," to give your followers an idea of when to expect new content.
* Use Instagram's built-in features, like the "Question" sticker or polls, to ask your followers about their preferences and adjust your posting schedule accordingly.

Remember, the most important thing is to find a rhythm that works for you and your writing process. Don't be afraid to experiment and adjust your approach as needed. Good luck, and have fun sharing your manuscript with your followers!
user: I think I'll try the hybrid approach, posting snippets of my manuscript as I complete new sections, but trying to maintain a consistent frequency. I'll also ask my followers about their preferences to see what they think. By the way, do you think I could use Instagram's "Poll" feature to ask my followers what they'd like to see more of in my content, like "More writing tips" or "More behind-the-scenes"?
assistant: The hybrid approach will give you the flexibility to share your manuscript snippets while maintaining a consistent presence on Instagram.

And absolutely, yes! The "Poll" feature is a great way to engage with your followers and gather feedback on what they'd like to see more of in your content. You can create a poll with options like:

* More writing tips and advice
* More behind-the-scenes content (e.g., writing process, research, etc.)
* More sneak peeks of your manuscript
* More personal stories and anecdotes
* Something else (with a comment section for them to share their ideas)

This will not only give you valuable insights into what your followers are interested in but also encourage them to engage with your content more actively. You can even use the results to inform your future content strategy and create more targeted posts that resonate with your audience.

Some additional tips for using the "Poll" feature effectively:

1. **Keep it simple**: Limit your options to 2-4 choices to make it easy for followers to decide.
2. **Make it relevant**: Ensure the poll is relevant to your content and audience to encourage engagement.
3. **Use attention-grabbing language**: Craft a compelling question or title to grab your followers' attention.
4. **Encourage sharing**: Ask followers to share their thoughts and opinions in the comments section to spark a discussion.
5. **Respond and engage**: Respond to comments and engage with followers who participate in the poll to build a sense of community.

By asking your followers for feedback, you'll demonstrate that you value their opinions and care about creating content that resonates with them. This can help increase engagement, loyalty, and even attract new followers who are interested in your writing and content.
user: I'm going to post a poll on my Instagram story asking my followers what they'd like to see more of in my content. I'll keep it simple with 3 options: "More writing tips and advice", "More behind-the-scenes content", and "More sneak peeks of my manuscript". I'll also ask them to share their thoughts and opinions in the comments section. Do you think I should share the results of the poll in a follow-up post or story to keep my followers engaged?
assistant: You're on a roll!

Sharing the results of the poll is a great idea to keep your followers engaged and invested in your content. Here's why:

1. **Closure**: By sharing the results, you're providing closure to the poll and giving your followers a sense of satisfaction that their opinions were heard.
2. **Transparency**: Sharing the results demonstrates transparency and shows that you value your followers' input.
3. **Encourages further engagement**: Sharing the results can spark further discussion and engagement, as followers may want to comment on the results or share their thoughts on why they voted a certain way.
4. **Informing future content**: By sharing the results, you're showing your followers that you're committed to creating content that resonates with them, which can increase their anticipation and engagement with future posts.

Here are some ideas for sharing the results:

1. **Follow-up story**: Create a follow-up story with a graphic or screenshot of the poll results, and add some context or insights about what you learned from the poll.
2. **Feed post**: Share a feed post with the results, and include some additional information about how you plan to incorporate the feedback into your content.
3. **IGTV or video**: Create a short IGTV or video discussing the results, and provide more in-depth insights into what you learned from the poll.
4. **Instagram Live**: Host an Instagram Live session to discuss the results, answer questions, and provide additional insights into your content strategy.

When sharing the results, be sure to:

* Thank your followers for participating and providing feedback
* Highlight the winning option (if one option received significantly more votes)
* Provide some insights into what you learned from the poll and how you plan to incorporate the feedback into your content
* Encourage further discussion and engagement by asking additional questions or requesting feedback on specific topics

By sharing the results, you'll demonstrate your commitment to creating content that resonates with your audience and keep them engaged with your brand.
user: I think I'll create a follow-up story with a graphic or screenshot of the poll results, and add some context or insights about what I learned from the poll. I'll also thank my followers for participating and providing feedback, and highlight the winning option. This way, I can keep my followers engaged and informed about the direction of my content. And who knows, maybe I'll even get some new ideas for my writing and content creation!
assistant: That's a great plan! By sharing the results of the poll, you'll not only show your followers that you value their opinions, but you'll also keep them engaged and invested in your content. The graphic or screenshot will make the results easy to understand, and adding context and insights will provide additional value to your followers.

By thanking your followers and highlighting the winning option, you'll demonstrate your appreciation for their participation and show that you're committed to creating content that resonates with them. This can help build trust and loyalty with your audience, which is essential for any writer or content creator.

And who knows, as you said, you might even get some new ideas for your writing and content creation! Sometimes, engaging with your audience and asking for feedback can lead to unexpected inspiration and insights.

Remember to keep your follow-up story concise, visually appealing, and engaging. You could even use this opportunity to tease some upcoming content or projects that align with the winning option. This will keep your followers excited and looking forward to what's next.

Good luck, and I hope you get some amazing feedback and insights from your followers!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.
<env>
Project path: gromozeka-e2e-<id>
Is directory a git repo: No
Platform: Mac OS X
OS Version: 26.5.1

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:1cea1afa

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains extractable durable profile/project facts and decisions plus richer reusable context about Instagram content strategy and audience engagement.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

- mention=Instagram; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=SERVICE; name=Instagram; reason=Instagram is a concrete reusable service mentioned as the platform for the user's social media content.
- mention=Instagram Stories / Instagram story; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=Instagram Stories; reason=Instagram Stories is a reusable product feature discussed as a content channel.

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/21 (Sun) 16:22
Transcript:
user: I've been trying to increase my social media presence, especially on Instagram. Can you help me come up with some ideas for more engaging promotional content, like the kind that will resonate with my audience and get more likes and comments? By the way, I just reached 500 followers last week, and I'm hoping to keep that momentum going.
assistant: Congratulations on reaching 500 followers! That's a great milestone! I'd be happy to help you come up with some engaging promotional content ideas that will resonate with your audience and help you continue to grow your following. Here are some suggestions:

**1. Behind-the-Scenes (BTS) Content**: Give your followers a sneak peek into your daily life, work process, or company culture. Share photos or videos of:
	* Your team's morning routine
	* Your design process or product development
	* A sneak peek of a new product or service
	* A company event or celebration

**2. User-Generated Content (UGC) Campaign**: Encourage your followers to share content related to your brand by:
	* Running a photo contest where followers share a photo using your product or service
	* Creating a branded hashtag and featuring the best content on your page
	* Collaborating with influencers or loyal customers to create content

**3. Educational or Informative Content**: Share valuable information related to your industry or niche, such as:
	* Tips and tricks related to your product or service
	* Industry news or updates
	* How-to guides or tutorials
	* Infographics or videos explaining complex concepts

**4. Personal Stories and Testimonials**: Share personal anecdotes or customer testimonials that showcase:
	* Your brand's mission and values
	* The impact of your product or service on customers' lives
	* Your team's passions and motivations

**5. Interactive Content**: Engage your followers with:
	* Quizzes or polls related to your industry or niche
	* "Ask Me Anything" (AMA) sessions or Q&A's
	* "Fill in the Blank" or "Caption This Photo" style posts

**6. Exclusive Offers and Giveaways**: Create buzz around your brand by offering:
	* Limited-time discounts or promo codes
	* Free trials or demos of your product or service
	* Giveaways or contests with prizes related to your industry

**7. Collaborations and Shoutouts**: Partner with other accounts or influencers in your industry to:
	* Reach new audiences
	* Create content together
	* Shoutout to other accounts and encourage them to do the same

**8. Seasonal and Timely Content**: Create content around holidays, trends, or current events, such as:
	* Holiday-themed posts or promotions
	* Seasonal tips or advice
	* Content related to trending topics or hashtags

**9. Sneak Peeks and Pre-Launch Hype**: Build anticipation for upcoming products or services by:
	* Sharing behind-the-scenes content
	* Teasing new features or benefits
	* Creating a countdown or launch calendar

**10. Authentic and Transparent Content**: Share your brand's personality and values by:
	* Being honest and transparent about your brand's journey
	* Sharing mistakes or lessons learned
	* Showcasing your brand's human side

Remember to keep your content visually appealing, engaging, and relevant to your audience. Mix and match these ideas to find what works best for your brand, and don't forget to track your analytics to see which content resonates with your followers.

What do you think? Is there a particular type of content that resonates with your audience, or do you have any specific questions about these ideas?
user: I think these are some great ideas, especially the behind-the-scenes content and user-generated content campaign. I've noticed that my followers love seeing photos of my cat, Luna, so maybe I can incorporate her into some BTS content. Do you think I could also use Instagram Stories to share some of my writing process, like snippets of my manuscript or research? And what are some good ways to encourage users to share UGC?
assistant: I'm glad you found the ideas helpful!

**Incorporating Luna into BTS content**: Absolutely! Pets can be a great way to humanize your brand and add a personal touch to your content. You could share photos or videos of Luna "supervising" your work, or even create a fun series like "Luna's Office Adventures" or "Luna's Writing Companion." This can help your followers connect with you on a more personal level and make your brand more relatable.

**Using Instagram Stories for writing process snippets**: Instagram Stories are a great way to share behind-the-scenes content, and your followers will love getting a glimpse into your writing process. You could share:

* Snippets of your manuscript or research notes
* Photos of your writing space or desk
* Videos of you brainstorming or outlining
* Quick peeks at your research materials or inspiration boards
* Even just a shot of your coffee cup or favorite writing snack

Remember to keep your Stories engaging and visually appealing, using text, stickers, and other features to make them fun and interactive. You can also use the "Question" sticker to ask your followers about their own writing processes or favorite books.

**Encouraging User-Generated Content (UGC)**: Here are some ways to encourage your followers to share UGC:

1. **Create a branded hashtag**: Designate a specific hashtag for your UGC campaign, and encourage followers to use it when sharing content related to your brand.
2. **Offer incentives**: Provide a reward or incentive for followers who share UGC, such as a discount code, freebie, or feature on your page.
3. **Make it easy to participate**: Provide a clear call-to-action (CTA) and make it easy for followers to share their content. You could even create a template or prompt to help them get started.
4. **Feature UGC regularly**: Showcase UGC on your page regularly to encourage others to participate and to show that you value their contributions.
5. **Engage with UGC**: Respond to and engage with UGC by liking, commenting, and sharing it. This will help create a sense of community and encourage more followers to participate.
6. **Run a contest or challenge**: Host a contest or challenge that encourages followers to share UGC, such as a photo contest or writing prompt challenge.
7. **Collaborate with influencers**: Partner with influencers or loyal followers to create UGC that showcases your brand in a unique and compelling way.

Some examples of UGC prompts for your writing-focused audience could be:

* "Share a photo of your favorite writing spot!"
* "What's the best writing advice you've ever received? Share it with us!"
* "Show us your writing process! Share a photo of your desk, notes, or research materials."
* "Write a short story or poem using the prompt [insert prompt] and share it with us!"

Remember to always keep your UGC campaign aligned with your brand's tone and values, and to respect your followers' content and intellectual property.
user: I like the idea of using Instagram Stories to share my writing process, and I think I'll start by posting some snippets of my manuscript. Do you think I should post these snippets as a series, like a "Manuscript Monday" feature, or just post them randomly as I complete new sections?
assistant: Both approaches have their pros and cons, so let's break them down:

**Series approach (e.g., "Manuscript Monday")**:

Pros:

* Consistency: Your followers will know when to expect new content from you, and they'll be more likely to engage with your Stories.
* Anticipation: A regular series can build anticipation and excitement around your manuscript.
* Organization: You can plan and prepare your content in advance, which can be helpful if you're busy with writing or other commitments.

Cons:

* Pressure: You might feel pressure to consistently produce new content, which could lead to burnout or decreased quality.
* Limited flexibility: If you're stuck on a particular section or need more time to revise, you might feel locked into a schedule that doesn't allow for flexibility.

**Random posting**:

Pros:

* Flexibility: You can post whenever you have new content ready, without feeling pressured by a schedule.
* Authenticity: Your followers will get a more authentic glimpse into your writing process, as you'll be sharing content as you work on it.
* Spontaneity: Random posting can create a sense of excitement and surprise, as your followers won't know when to expect new content.

Cons:

* Unpredictability: Your followers might not know when to expect new content, which could lead to decreased engagement.
* Inconsistency: Without a regular schedule, you might post less frequently, which could impact your overall engagement and momentum.

Consider a hybrid approach:

* Post snippets of your manuscript as you complete new sections, but try to maintain a consistent frequency (e.g., 2-3 times a week).
* Create a loose schedule or theme, like "Writing Wednesday" or "Friday Fiction," to give your followers an idea of when to expect new content.
* Use Instagram's built-in features, like the "Question" sticker or polls, to ask your followers about their preferences and adjust your posting schedule accordingly.

Remember, the most important thing is to find a rhythm that works for you and your writing process. Don't be afraid to experiment and adjust your approach as needed. Good luck, and have fun sharing your manuscript with your followers!
user: I think I'll try the hybrid approach, posting snippets of my manuscript as I complete new sections, but trying to maintain a consistent frequency. I'll also ask my followers about their preferences to see what they think. By the way, do you think I could use Instagram's "Poll" feature to ask my followers what they'd like to see more of in my content, like "More writing tips" or "More behind-the-scenes"?
assistant: The hybrid approach will give you the flexibility to share your manuscript snippets while maintaining a consistent presence on Instagram.

And absolutely, yes! The "Poll" feature is a great way to engage with your followers and gather feedback on what they'd like to see more of in your content. You can create a poll with options like:

* More writing tips and advice
* More behind-the-scenes content (e.g., writing process, research, etc.)
* More sneak peeks of your manuscript
* More personal stories and anecdotes
* Something else (with a comment section for them to share their ideas)

This will not only give you valuable insights into what your followers are interested in but also encourage them to engage with your content more actively. You can even use the results to inform your future content strategy and create more targeted posts that resonate with your audience.

Some additional tips for using the "Poll" feature effectively:

1. **Keep it simple**: Limit your options to 2-4 choices to make it easy for followers to decide.
2. **Make it relevant**: Ensure the poll is relevant to your content and audience to encourage engagement.
3. **Use attention-grabbing language**: Craft a compelling question or title to grab your followers' attention.
4. **Encourage sharing**: Ask followers to share their thoughts and opinions in the comments section to spark a discussion.
5. **Respond and engage**: Respond to comments and engage with followers who participate in the poll to build a sense of community.

By asking your followers for feedback, you'll demonstrate that you value their opinions and care about creating content that resonates with them. This can help increase engagement, loyalty, and even attract new followers who are interested in your writing and content.
user: I'm going to post a poll on my Instagram story asking my followers what they'd like to see more of in my content. I'll keep it simple with 3 options: "More writing tips and advice", "More behind-the-scenes content", and "More sneak peeks of my manuscript". I'll also ask them to share their thoughts and opinions in the comments section. Do you think I should share the results of the poll in a follow-up post or story to keep my followers engaged?
assistant: You're on a roll!

Sharing the results of the poll is a great idea to keep your followers engaged and invested in your content. Here's why:

1. **Closure**: By sharing the results, you're providing closure to the poll and giving your followers a sense of satisfaction that their opinions were heard.
2. **Transparency**: Sharing the results demonstrates transparency and shows that you value your followers' input.
3. **Encourages further engagement**: Sharing the results can spark further discussion and engagement, as followers may want to comment on the results or share their thoughts on why they voted a certain way.
4. **Informing future content**: By sharing the results, you're showing your followers that you're committed to creating content that resonates with them, which can increase their anticipation and engagement with future posts.

Here are some ideas for sharing the results:

1. **Follow-up story**: Create a follow-up story with a graphic or screenshot of the poll results, and add some context or insights about what you learned from the poll.
2. **Feed post**: Share a feed post with the results, and include some additional information about how you plan to incorporate the feedback into your content.
3. **IGTV or video**: Create a short IGTV or video discussing the results, and provide more in-depth insights into what you learned from the poll.
4. **Instagram Live**: Host an Instagram Live session to discuss the results, answer questions, and provide additional insights into your content strategy.

When sharing the results, be sure to:

* Thank your followers for participating and providing feedback
* Highlight the winning option (if one option received significantly more votes)
* Provide some insights into what you learned from the poll and how you plan to incorporate the feedback into your content
* Encourage further discussion and engagement by asking additional questions or requesting feedback on specific topics

By sharing the results, you'll demonstrate your commitment to creating content that resonates with your audience and keep them engaged with your brand.
user: I think I'll create a follow-up story with a graphic or screenshot of the poll results, and add some context or insights about what I learned from the poll. I'll also thank my followers for participating and providing feedback, and highlight the winning option. This way, I can keep my followers engaged and informed about the direction of my content. And who knows, maybe I'll even get some new ideas for my writing and content creation!
assistant: That's a great plan! By sharing the results of the poll, you'll not only show your followers that you value their opinions, but you'll also keep them engaged and invested in your content. The graphic or screenshot will make the results easy to understand, and adding context and insights will provide additional value to your followers.

By thanking your followers and highlighting the winning option, you'll demonstrate your appreciation for their participation and show that you're committed to creating content that resonates with them. This can help build trust and loyalty with your audience, which is essential for any writer or content creator.

And who knows, as you said, you might even get some new ideas for your writing and content creation! Sometimes, engaging with your audience and asking for feedback can lead to unexpected inspiration and insights.

Remember to keep your follow-up story concise, visually appealing, and engaging. You could even use this opportunity to tease some upcoming content or projects that align with the winning option. This will keep your followers excited and looking forward to what's next.

Good luck, and I hope you get some amazing feedback and insights from your followers!
